content.text: An inner-source contribution model for agent tools is an organizational pattern that applies open-source collaboration practices to internally developed shared code: agent tool libraries, prompt templates, eval fixture datasets, and integration SDKs. Under this model, these shared assets are hosted in internal repositories with open access to any engineer in the organization. Any team can contribute improvements, bug fixes, or new capabilities through a pull request process. Contributions are reviewed by designated trusted committers and maintainers who are accountable for the quality and direction of the shared asset. Accepted contributions are released as versioned packages available to all teams.

The inner-source model is distinguished from simple code sharing by the formalization of roles and processes. Without role clarity, repositories that are nominally open for contribution quickly become either contributor-hostile (every PR lingers unreviewed for months) or quality-diluting (any PR is merged without substantive review). Inner-source provides the structure of an open-source project — CONTRIBUTING.md, code of conduct, defined reviewer roles, documented quality bar — adapted for the internal context where contributors and maintainers are colleagues with shared organizational goals.

Agent tool development involves repetitive, cross-cutting concerns: web search integrations, document retrieval, API client wrappers, output parsers, eval harnesses. Without inner-source, each team implements these independently, spending engineering time on undifferentiated infrastructure rather than on their specific agent capability. The implementations diverge, accumulate different bugs, and cannot be maintained centrally. When a security issue is found in one team's API client wrapper, there is no mechanism to apply the fix to the other teams' copies.

Inner-source creates a flywheel: the more teams contribute to shared agent tool libraries, the more capable and well-tested those libraries become, the more useful they are to adopt, and the more teams adopt them and eventually contribute back. This flywheel is self-reinforcing when the contribution process is low-friction and the review response time is short. It stalls when reviews take weeks, when contribution guidelines are unclear, or when contributors feel their PRs are unwelcome.
Teams contribute improvements they need rather than duplicating work across isolated codebases.
Shared asset quality improves continuously as multiple teams identify and fix bugs they encounter in production.
Security patches in shared tool libraries propagate to all consumers through the package release process.
Contributors develop cross-team relationships and shared understanding of platform patterns.
The platform team's maintenance burden is distributed across trusted committers from multiple product teams.
Inner-source contribution history is evidence of organizational knowledge sharing that can be cited in engineering reviews.
Establishing and maintaining trusted committer roles requires investment from platform team and from the committers themselves.
Poorly managed inner-source repos accumulate feature requests and stale PRs that erode contributor morale.
Contributions from teams with different technical expertise and standards require careful review to maintain quality.
Governance overhead increases with the number of inner-source repositories; not every shared library merits the full model.
Contributors from teams without dedicated time for inner-source work will contribute less consistently, creating uneven participation.

The platform team designates a set of shared repositories as inner-source projects and publishes the program's structure, roles, and expectations. Each inner-source repository has a CONTRIBUTING.md that documents: how to set up a local development environment, the coding style and test requirements for contributions, the PR review process and expected response time from trusted committers, the criteria for becoming a trusted committer, and the escalation path when a contribution is blocked or disputed.

Contributors from any team submit PRs to inner-source repositories following the documented contribution guidelines. Trusted committers from one or more teams provide substantive review and either approve the PR, request changes, or explain why the contribution does not align with the repository's direction. The maintainer makes the final merge decision and triggers the release process. Contributors who demonstrate sustained, high-quality contributions over time are invited to become trusted committers, giving them merge authority and direct influence over the project's direction.

The platform team provides the governance infrastructure: the internal package registry where releases are published, the CI/CD system that runs tests and security scans on every PR, the metrics dashboard that tracks contribution volume, review time, and active contributors per repository, and the program-level forums (a dedicated Slack channel, quarterly all-hands) where inner-source participants discuss cross-repository concerns and recognize contributions.

Apply inner-source to shared code that is used by multiple teams, is likely to receive improvement contributions from those teams, and is sufficiently complex to warrant a structured review process. Tool integration libraries, prompt template libraries, and eval fixture datasets all meet these criteria. Simple utility functions or scripts that are copied between a few teams do not require the full inner-source model; a well-commented shared module with a single team's ownership is often sufficient.

Resist the impulse to inner-source every shared library from the start. Begin inner-source with one or two repositories that have the strongest cross-team interest and a willing initial maintainer. A well-run inner-source program with two repositories builds more organizational trust than a poorly run one with twenty. Expand to additional repositories after the model is working well in the initial set.

# CONTRIBUTING.md — template for inner-source agent tool libraries

## Getting started

Clone the repository and install development dependencies:

```bash
git clone https://github.internal.company.com/platform/agent-tool-library
cd agent-tool-library
pip install -e ".[dev]"
pre-commit install
```

## Making a contribution

1. Open an issue describing the bug or feature before starting significant work.
   This lets maintainers confirm the contribution aligns with the project direction.
2. Fork the repository and create a branch: `git checkout -b your-name/short-description`.
3. Write your change with:
   - Unit tests covering the new behavior (coverage must not decrease).
   - Docstrings on all public functions.
   - An entry in CHANGELOG.md under the [Unreleased] section.
4. Run the full test suite: `pytest && mypy src/`
5. Open a PR with a clear description of the problem and solution.

## Review process

- A trusted committer will review your PR within 5 business days.
- Expect one or two review rounds before merge.
- If your PR is not reviewed within 5 days, ping @platform-team in #inner-source.

## Becoming a trusted committer

Trusted committers are engineers who have made 3+ merged contributions and
demonstrated understanding of the project's design goals. Nominations are
accepted from any team. Contact @maintainer-team to express interest.

# Example: contributing a new tool to agent-tool-library
# src/agent_tool_library/tools/web_scraper.py

from typing import Optional
import httpx
from ..base import BaseTool, ToolResult

class WebScraperTool(BaseTool):
    """Fetches and extracts readable text from a web page.

    This tool is suitable for agent workflows that need to read publicly
    accessible web content. It does not handle JavaScript-rendered content.

    Args:
        max_length: Maximum character length of extracted text (default 4000).
        timeout_seconds: HTTP request timeout (default 10).
    """

    def __init__(self, max_length: int = 4000, timeout_seconds: float = 10.0):
        self.max_length = max_length
        self.timeout = timeout_seconds

    def run(self, url: str, *, selector: Optional[str] = None) -> ToolResult:
        """Fetch and extract text from a URL.

        Args:
            url: The URL to fetch. Must be an http or https URL.
            selector: Optional CSS selector to extract a specific section.

        Returns:
            ToolResult with extracted text or an error message.
        """
        if not url.startswith(("http://", "https://")):
            return ToolResult.error(f"Invalid URL scheme: {url!r}")
        try:
            response = httpx.get(url, timeout=self.timeout, follow_redirects=True)
            response.raise_for_status()
            # Extract text (simplified — real implementation uses BeautifulSoup)
            text = response.text[:self.max_length]
            return ToolResult.ok(text)
        except httpx.HTTPError as e:
            return ToolResult.error(f"HTTP error fetching {url}: {e}")

# .github/workflows/ci.yml — CI for inner-source repo
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: ["3.10", "3.11", "3.12"]  # test all supported versions
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: pytest --cov=src --cov-fail-under=90
      - run: mypy src/
      - run: semgrep --config=p/python ci  # security scan

Team A builds a Slack notification tool for their alert-routing agent. Rather than keeping it private in their service repository, they contribute it to the inner-source agent-tool-library. They open an issue describing the tool, receive a positive response from the maintainer within two days, implement the tool following the contribution guidelines (with tests, docstring, and changelog entry), and submit a PR. A trusted committer reviews within four days, requests one change (use the existing retry utility rather than a custom implementation), and approves after the change is made. The tool is merged and released in v2.4.0.

Three months later, Team C discovers the Slack notification tool while browsing the library and adopts it. They find a bug: the tool does not handle Slack rate limiting correctly when sending many notifications in quick succession. Rather than fixing it only in their code, they contribute the fix back to the library. Their PR includes a regression test for the rate limiting scenario. The fix is reviewed, merged, and released in v2.4.1, which all teams pinned to the 2.x range receive automatically via Renovate upgrade PRs.

The platform team reviews inner-source metrics quarterly. They notice that the prompt-templates repository has a high number of open PRs with long review time, indicating that the current trusted committers are under-resourced. They reach out to a senior engineer on Team D who has been an active contributor and invite them to become a trusted committer. With the additional reviewer, the average PR review time drops from 12 days to 4 days, and contributor submission volume increases as the faster feedback loop encourages more participation.

PayPal's inner-source program, documented in their engineering blog, is one of the most cited examples of the model at scale. PayPal established inner-source practices across hundreds of repositories, with the InnerSource Commons organization (innersourcecommons.org) emerging from PayPal alumni sharing their learnings publicly. The commons documents the trusted committer role, the CONTRIBUTING.md pattern, and the organizational governance model in their InnerSource Patterns collection. These patterns are directly applicable to agent tool libraries: the Slack notification tool contribution example above mirrors real patterns from PayPal's inner-source library ecosystem, adapted to the agent AI context.
Starting inner-source without committing to review SLA enforcement; unreviewed PRs that linger for months kill contributor motivation faster than any other failure mode.
Making the CONTRIBUTING.md too complex, requiring contributors to understand platform internals before they can submit a simple bug fix.
Not recognizing contributors publicly; recognition (changelog credit, shout-outs in team forums) is a primary motivator for voluntary inner-source participation.
Treating trusted committer as a title without ongoing obligation; committers who stop reviewing should be transitioned to emeritus status so active reviewers can be identified.
Inner-sourcing a library that has no demand from other teams; not every library warrants the overhead of the full model.
Allowing the platform team to become the sole committer for inner-source repos, recreating the bottleneck that inner-source is meant to eliminate.
Publish a 5-business-day review SLA and enforce it; if a PR is not reviewed within the SLA, the maintainer team is responsible for escalation.
Acknowledge every issue and PR with an initial response within 24 hours, even if full review will take longer.
Credit contributors in release changelogs and celebrate inner-source milestones (first external contribution, hundredth merged PR) in org-wide forums.
Run regular inner-source office hours where contributors can ask questions and get live feedback before investing in a large PR.
Make the contribution path incremental: documentation fixes and test additions are valid contributions that build familiarity before a contributor attempts a new feature.
Set a minimum test coverage floor and enforce it in CI; do not make exceptions for contributions from senior engineers.

The inner-source model scales well with team count but requires increasing governance investment as the number of repositories and contributors grows. A program with 5 repositories and 20 active contributors can be governed informally through a dedicated Slack channel and a shared metrics spreadsheet. A program with 30 repositories and 200 contributors requires a dedicated program manager, automated metrics dashboards, and formal quarterly reviews of each repository's health. Plan governance capacity proportional to program scope.

Review throughput is the primary bottleneck in a growing inner-source program. The trusted committer role distributes review work across the organization, but trusted committers are doing inner-source review alongside their primary team responsibilities. Measure review throughput per trusted committer and create clear off-ramps for committers who can no longer maintain the review SLA, so that inactive committers do not create false expectations for contributors.
Run on all PRs from any fork
PR volume, review time, contributor count

Inner-source contribution creates a code review requirement for all external contributions to shared libraries. The CI/CD pipeline must run security scans (SAST, dependency vulnerability checks) on every PR from any contributor before merge, not just on PRs from the platform team. Trusted committers should be trained to identify common security issues in agent tool code: injection risks in URL construction, credential handling in API client code, and unsafe deserialization in output parsers. The contribution process should explicitly require that security-sensitive changes receive review from at least one security-aware trusted committer.

Reliability of inner-source libraries affects all teams that depend on them. A breaking release or a regression in a widely-used library is an organization-wide incident. The release process must include automated integration tests that catch regressions before publication, and the version management policy must ensure that breaking changes are only released in major versions with advance notice. Post-release monitoring should alert the maintainer team if a new release correlates with an unusual rate of bug reports from consuming services.
Inner-source applies open-source collaboration practices to internal shared code, enabling any team to contribute rather than only the owning team.
Three distinct roles drive inner-source: contributors (any engineer), trusted committers (reviewers who guard quality), and maintainers (owners of direction and releases).
A 5-business-day review SLA enforced by the maintainer team is the most important operational commitment; unreviewed PRs are the primary killer of contributor motivation.
Recognition through changelog credits, forum shout-outs, and trusted committer invitations sustains voluntary participation.
Governance investment must scale with program scope; informal governance breaks down past approximately 10 repositories.
Security scans and integration tests must run on PRs from all contributors, not just the platform team, before merge is permitted.
